pages.text: HE SOU ' 360 nes THE SOUTHERN AFRICA INDIAN WHO'S WHO HE AOLTHER SARMAUNCIAN WHOS WHO : =I HAFFEJEE'S FURNITURE MANUFACTURERS ‘The first of the Haffejes to arrive in this country was Hoosen KhatiD who embarked from the island of Mauritius, on board the S.S. Attia, in 1888. He Settled in Durban for a while where he had a trading store in Gardiner Street, opposite the General Post Office. He later moved to lxopo and over the years, | ished several branches in country areas, at Bizana and Howick, Natal. He | in the 30's, He died in 1931] Mahmood Hoosen Haffejee, the fourth son of Hoosen Khatib, was born at Ixopo in 1899, He moved to Durban and established a light furniture factory in 1914 in the central area. During the depression he had several hundred workers in his two adjoining factories in Lorne Street and specialized in shopfitting and mass-produced furniture. He was a pioneer amongst the early Muslims in the furniture field. He later moved to Kilkenny Road, Mayville and in 1954, to Bellair Road, Cato Manor. Expropriation, however. had a considerable effect on further expansion and the factory continues under Council tenancy to this day, until new premises are built in the near future in the Isipingo industrial complex. In 1948, Ahmed Haffejee, the eldest son of Mahmood Hoosen Haffejee, joined his father in business and by 1958 had added a new division to the factory. ‘The manufacturing of Kitchen furniture was undertaken and the expansion has continued to such an extent that the Hafiejee Furniture Manufacturers is now acknowledged amongst the top 5 in the Republic in this field. The factory con- tinues to manufacture its other products. Alter the death of his father in 1968 Ahmed Haffejee assumed complete control of the manufacturing concern. He was joined by his brother, Hafez Mohamed Hoosen, who has now become the Production Manager and is also a partner in the firm, The firm produces close on a thousand Kitchen units each month and the factory turn-out of other furniture products puts it ahead of all its rivals, Wooden suitcases and trunks manufactured by the firm are directed to the lucrative Pondo trade as well as to local needs. The little factory started by Mahmood Hoosen Hatfejee in 1914 is today making its contribution to the industrial development of this province and with the expansion envisaged, for the not too distant future, will continue to play its part in an ever expanding Natal economy.
